name: AWS Textract
what_it_does: ML-powered document analysis service that extracts text, tables, forms, signatures, and structured data from PDFs and images — going beyond OCR to understand document layout and semantics.
use_cases: Agents processing invoices, receipts, or purchase orders to extract line items and totals into structured data
Automating form ingestion (W-2s, insurance claims, medical records) by extracting field-value pairs
Document pipeline pre-processing — converting scanned PDFs to structured JSON before feeding to an LLM
Extracting tables from financial reports or research PDFs for downstream analysis
not_for: Simple plain-text PDFs — standard PDF parsing libraries (pdfplumber, PyMuPDF) are faster and cheaper
Handwritten text with low legibility — accuracy drops significantly vs. printed text
Real-time document processing with sub-second latency requirements — async API adds overhead for multi-page documents
best_when: You need to extract structured data (tables, key-value form fields) from scanned or photographed documents, not just raw text, and document layout understanding matters.
avoid_when: Your documents are well-formed digital PDFs with embedded text — Textract adds cost and latency without benefit over direct text extraction.

auth.notes: AWS SigV4 signing via IAM credentials or roles. Synchronous API (DetectDocumentText, AnalyzeDocument) requires textract:DetectDocumentText or textract:AnalyzeDocument. Async operations (StartDocumentAnalysis) additionally require SNS and S3 permissions for result delivery.

pricing.free_tier_limits: 1,000 pages/month for DetectDocumentText and AnalyzeDocument (Forms and Tables) for the first 3 months.
pricing.paid_tiers: DetectDocumentText: $1.50/1,000 pages. AnalyzeDocument (Forms): $50/1,000 pages. AnalyzeDocument (Tables): $15/1,000 pages. Queries feature: $40/1,000 pages + $0.01/query.
pricing.notes: Forms and Tables features cost significantly more than basic text detection. Async jobs for large documents use the same per-page pricing. Input documents must be stored in S3 for async operations.

performance.rate_limits: Synchronous: 100 TPS (DetectDocumentText), 100 TPS (AnalyzeDocument). Async: 2 concurrent jobs by default, increase via Service Quotas. Max 15 pages for synchronous; unlimited for async.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Synchronous API errors are well-structured. Async job failures surface in the GetDocumentAnalysis response JobStatus field, not as exceptions — agents must check status rather than catching errors.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Each API call triggers a new analysis job with a unique JobId. No built-in deduplication — agents submitting the same document twice will be charged twice. Implement S3-based caching keyed on document hash.
known_agent_gotchas: Async API (required for multi-page documents) requires polling GetDocumentAnalysis — there is no push callback without configuring SNS notifications
Document input for async jobs must be in S3 in the same region as the Textract call — cross-region S3 will fail
The response JSON for complex documents can be very large (MBs) — agents should process blocks incrementally rather than loading the full response
Table extraction quality varies significantly with table complexity; merged cells and nested tables reduce accuracy
Concurrent async job limit defaults to 2 — this is a hard bottleneck for agent pipelines processing many documents simultaneously

security_score_components.security_notes: Documents are processed in AWS's isolated compute — not stored by Textract. Input must be in S3; server-side encryption (SSE-KMS) on the S3 bucket protects data at rest. VPC endpoints available for private network access. HIPAA-eligible for healthcare document workflows.
